Practical Machine Learning - Course Project

Rafael Del Grande

2026-03-08

Overview

This report describes the development of a machine learning model to predict the manner in which participants performed barbell lifts, using accelerometer data from belt, forearm, arm, and dumbbell sensors. The outcome variable classe has 5 levels (A–E), where A represents correct execution and B–E represent common mistakes.

Data Loading & Cleaning

library(caret)
library(randomForest)
library(ggplot2)
library(corrplot)

# Load data
training_url <- "https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/predmachlearn/pml-training.csv"
testing_url  <- "https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/predmachlearn/pml-testing.csv"

training_raw <- read.csv(training_url, na.strings = c("NA", "", "#DIV/0!"))
testing_raw  <- read.csv(testing_url,  na.strings = c("NA", "", "#DIV/0!"))

cat("Training dimensions:", dim(training_raw), "\n")
## Training dimensions: 19622 160
cat("Testing dimensions: ", dim(testing_raw),  "\n")
## Testing dimensions:  20 160
# Remove columns with > 95% NA values
na_threshold <- 0.95
na_cols <- colMeans(is.na(training_raw)) > na_threshold
training_clean <- training_raw[, !na_cols]
testing_clean  <- testing_raw[,  !na_cols]

# Remove identification columns (cols 1-7: name, timestamps, window info)
training_clean <- training_clean[, -(1:7)]
testing_clean  <- testing_clean[,  -(1:7)]

# Remove near-zero variance predictors
nzv <- nearZeroVar(training_clean)
if (length(nzv) > 0) {
  training_clean <- training_clean[, -nzv]
  testing_clean  <- testing_clean[,  -nzv]
}

# Make classe a factor
training_clean$classe <- as.factor(training_clean$classe)

cat("Cleaned training dimensions:", dim(training_clean), "\n")
## Cleaned training dimensions: 19622 53
cat("Remaining predictors:", ncol(training_clean) - 1, "\n")
## Remaining predictors: 52

Exploratory Data Analysis

# Distribution of outcome variable
ggplot(training_clean, aes(x = classe, fill = classe)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Classe Variable",
       x = "Classe", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

# Correlation matrix of first 20 numeric predictors
num_cols <- sapply(training_clean[, -ncol(training_clean)], is.numeric)
cor_matrix <- cor(training_clean[, num_cols][, 1:20])
corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
         tl.cex = 0.6, title = "Correlation Matrix (First 20 Predictors)",
         mar = c(0,0,1,0))

Data Partitioning

set.seed(12345)

# Split training data into train/validation (75/25)
inTrain <- createDataPartition(training_clean$classe, p = 0.75, list = FALSE)
train_set <- training_clean[inTrain,  ]
valid_set <- training_clean[-inTrain, ]

cat("Train set size:     ", nrow(train_set), "\n")
## Train set size:      14718
cat("Validation set size:", nrow(valid_set), "\n")
## Validation set size: 4904

Model Building

Cross-Validation Setup

# 5-fold cross-validation to balance accuracy and computation
control <- trainControl(
  method          = "cv",
  number          = 5,
  verboseIter     = FALSE,
  allowParallel   = TRUE
)

Model 1: Random Forest

set.seed(12345)
model_rf <- train(classe ~ .,
                  data      = train_set,
                  method    = "rf",
                  trControl = control,
                  ntree     = 200)

# Validate
pred_rf   <- predict(model_rf, valid_set)
cm_rf     <- confusionMatrix(pred_rf, valid_set$classe)
acc_rf    <- cm_rf$overall["Accuracy"]

cat("Random Forest Accuracy:", round(acc_rf, 4), "\n")
## Random Forest Accuracy: 0.9943
print(cm_rf$table)
##           Reference
## Prediction    A    B    C    D    E
##          A 1394    1    0    0    0
##          B    0  947    2    0    1
##          C    1    1  849   10    2
##          D    0    0    4  789    1
##          E    0    0    0    5  897

Model 2: Gradient Boosting (GBM)

set.seed(12345)
model_gbm <- train(classe ~ .,
                   data      = train_set,
                   method    = "gbm",
                   trControl = control,
                   verbose   = FALSE)

# Validate
pred_gbm  <- predict(model_gbm, valid_set)
cm_gbm    <- confusionMatrix(pred_gbm, valid_set$classe)
acc_gbm   <- cm_gbm$overall["Accuracy"]

cat("GBM Accuracy:", round(acc_gbm, 4), "\n")
## GBM Accuracy: 0.9621
print(cm_gbm$table)
##           Reference
## Prediction    A    B    C    D    E
##          A 1376   24    0    2    0
##          B   16  896   25    2   10
##          C    1   29  816   32   11
##          D    2    0   14  761   11
##          E    0    0    0    7  869

Model Comparison

results <- data.frame(
  Model    = c("Random Forest", "GBM"),
  Accuracy = c(round(acc_rf, 4), round(acc_gbm, 4)),
  OOS_Error = c(round(1 - acc_rf, 4), round(1 - acc_gbm, 4))
)
print(results)
##           Model Accuracy OOS_Error
## 1 Random Forest   0.9943    0.0057
## 2           GBM   0.9621    0.0379

Final Model Selection & Variable Importance

Random Forest is selected as the final model based on highest accuracy.

# Variable importance plot
importance <- varImp(model_rf)
plot(importance,
     top  = 20,
     main = "Top 20 Variable Importance - Random Forest")

Expected Out-of-Sample Error

cat("=== Final Model: Random Forest ===\n")
## === Final Model: Random Forest ===
cat("Validation Accuracy:       ", round(acc_rf, 4), "\n")
## Validation Accuracy:        0.9943
cat("Expected OOS Error:        ", round(1 - acc_rf, 4), "\n")
## Expected OOS Error:         0.0057
cat("Expected OOS Error (%):    ", round((1 - acc_rf) * 100, 2), "%\n\n")
## Expected OOS Error (%):     0.57 %
cat("Cross-Validation Results:\n")
## Cross-Validation Results:
print(model_rf$resample)
##    Accuracy     Kappa Resample
## 1 0.9921822 0.9901092    Fold1
## 2 0.9942255 0.9926955    Fold3
## 3 0.9921822 0.9901108    Fold2
## 4 0.9938879 0.9922690    Fold5
## 5 0.9853990 0.9815329    Fold4
cat("\nMean CV Accuracy:", round(mean(model_rf$resample$Accuracy), 4), "\n")
## 
## Mean CV Accuracy: 0.9916

Why Random Forest?

  • High accuracy: Typically >99% on this dataset
  • Handles non-linearity: No assumptions about predictor relationships
  • Built-in feature selection: Via variable importance
  • Robust to overfitting: Via bagging across many trees
  • Cross-validation: 5-fold CV used to tune mtry parameter

Predictions on 20 Test Cases

# Final predictions on the 20 test cases
final_predictions <- predict(model_rf, testing_clean)

cat("Predictions for 20 test cases:\n")
## Predictions for 20 test cases:
results_df <- data.frame(
  Case       = 1:20,
  Prediction = as.character(final_predictions)
)
print(results_df)
##    Case Prediction
## 1     1          B
## 2     2          A
## 3     3          B
## 4     4          A
## 5     5          A
## 6     6          E
## 7     7          D
## 8     8          B
## 9     9          A
## 10   10          A
## 11   11          B
## 12   12          C
## 13   13          B
## 14   14          A
## 15   15          E
## 16   16          E
## 17   17          A
## 18   18          B
## 19   19          B
## 20   20          B

Conclusion

The Random Forest model achieved:

Metric Value
Validation Accuracy ~99%
Expected OOS Error ~1%
Cross-Validation 5-fold
Predictors Used 52

Key findings: - Belt sensors (roll_belt, yaw_belt) were the most important predictors - Random Forest significantly outperformed GBM on this dataset - The low OOS error (~1%) gives high confidence in the 20 test case predictions
